stem: Find the distance from the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) to the line \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y+([[c]])=0 \).
Find the distance from the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) to the line \( y=[[m]]x+[[k]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root in the denominator.
This flips the sign of \( y_0 \) when converting to general form.
This forgets to divide by \( \sqrt{m^2+1}\) entirely.
This divides by \( m\) instead of \( \sqrt{m^2+1}\).
This is off by one from the correct distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct distance.

stem: Find the distance between the parallel lines \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y+([[c1]])=0 \) and \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y+([[c2]])=0 \).
solution: For parallel lines \( ax+by+c_1=0 \) and \( ax+by+c_2=0 \), the distance between them is \( \dfrac{|c_1-c_2|}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root in the denominator.
This adds \( c_1\) and \( c_2\) instead of subtracting them.
This forgets to divide by \( \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\) entirely.
This inverts the correct ratio.
This is off by one from the correct distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct distance.

stem: Find the distance from the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) to the line through \( ([[x1]],[[y1]]) \) and \( ([[x2]],[[y2]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root in the denominator.
This flips the sign of the constant term \( c\).
This forgets the constant term \( c\) entirely.
This computes the distance to one of the defining points instead of to the line itself.
This is off by one from the correct distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct distance.

stem: Find the distance from the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) to the vertical line \( x=[[k]] \).
solution: For a vertical line \( x=k \), the distance from any point \( (x_0,y_0) \) is simply the horizontal gap \( |x_0-k|=[[answer]] \); the \( y \)-coordinate is irrelevant.
distractor_rationales: This uses the point's \( y\)-coordinate instead of its \( x\)-coordinate.
This drops the absolute value, which can be negative.
This adds instead of subtracts before taking the absolute value.
This mistakenly includes the \( y\)-coordinate as if the line were not vertical.
This is off by one from the correct distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct distance.

stem: A line has \( x \)-intercept \( [[p]] \) and \( y \)-intercept \( [[q]] \), so its equation is \( \dfrac{x}{[[p]]}+\dfrac{y}{[[q]]}=1 \). Find the distance from the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) to this line.
solution: Clearing denominators, \( \frac{x}{p}+\frac{y}{q}=1 \) becomes \( qx+py-pq=0 \), so the distance is \( \dfrac{|q([[x0]])+p([[y0]])-pq|}{\sqrt{p^{2}+q^{2}}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root in the denominator.
This swaps the coefficients \( p\) and \( q\) when clearing denominators.
This forgets the constant term \( -pq\) entirely.
This flips the sign of the constant term \( -pq\).
This is off by one from the correct distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct distance.

stem: The distance from the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) to the line \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y+k=0 \) is \( [[d]] \). Which of the following is a possible value of \( k \)?
solution: Since \( \sqrt{[[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2}}=[[c]] \), the distance condition is \( |[[a]]([[x0]])+[[b]]([[y0]])+k|=[[d]]\cdot[[c]] \); solving for \( k \) gives \( [[answer]] \) as one solution.
distractor_rationales: This is off by a small additive amount from a genuine solution.
This uses the other sign of the absolute value but is then also off by one.
This uses \( d\) instead of \( d\cdot c\) (forgetting \( \sqrt{a^2+b^2}=c\)).
This reports \( d\cdot c\) itself rather than solving for \( k\).
This drops the distance term \( d\cdot c\) entirely.
This is off by a different small additive amount from a genuine solution.
